comments.author: Steve
comments.text: The only lie in this statement is that the codebase should eventually be the sidecars. The language of the system should eventually be the same across all three codebases, and it should be for Claude. The implementation choices so far have been decided by me (with lots of consultation from me), but the code itself isn't the architecture.  It's the system design.  We realistically don't need 3 codebases for single-player Lyn Rummy, as I already demonstrated in my TS implementation. We didn't even need it for two-player games; I was able to finagle a wire format using really janky Zulip messages as the transport and the database. With Claude, we were able to introduce Go as the transport first. Things immediately improved. On the Python side, that was always Claude's decision. Claude chose Python before I ever asked Claude. Ironically, Python is the language that I am most fluent in. And we didn't have any agent capabilities until Claude arrived on the scene.

comments.author: Steve
comments.text: When we come back to Lyn Rummy, there is still a HUGE problem to solve, which is actual two-person play. I think this is when the three-tier architecture will begin to shine.
